course_description: This course is an introductory level examination of the basic principles of taxation law in Australia, with particular emphasis on the topics required by professional accounting bodies for accreditation purposes.

requirements.restrictions: Not for students enrolled in LLB(Hons)(#64), LLB(Hons)(#48), LLB, LLB (Grad Entry), LLB(Hons) duals and LLB dual programs
requirements.incompatible: LAWS5144 or LAWS7012 or LAWS7144 or LAWS7344
requirements.assumed_background: Students are assumed to have studied and passed LAWS1100 Business Law (or its equivalent) and to have retained from that course an understanding of the Australian legal system, the ability to apply legal knowledge and legal reasoning in the analysis and resolution of legal problems, and an understanding of the core differences between sole trader businesses, partnerships and companies. In particular, the nature of the ownership rights in sole trader businesses, partnerships and companies are relevant to income tax law.

timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Additional Timetable Information
Lectures

Lectures will be recorded and posted to the Learning Resources folder in the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ (Blackboard).

Tutorials

From Week 4, a tutorial class recording will be released to the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ for each separate tutorial - see the week-by-week program. The purpose of these recordings is to share the answers to tutorials.

From Week 4, a recording will be released every week that reflects on student approaches to answering tutorial questions. See the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ for the week-by-week program.

Podcasts

During the semester, podcasts will be posted to the course site on Learn.UQ. These podcasts take the form of voice-over-PowerPoint-slides, and are thus easily accessible. Podcasts form an integral part of the learning resources in the course.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims:

To equip students with sufficient knowledge in Australian income tax law to enable them to deal with clients, business persons and legal advisers in an informed and reasoned manner.

To demonstrate the development of legal principles through case law and statutory provisions.

To equip students with the skill and technique to calculate taxable income, tax payable, Fringe Benefits Tax payable and Goods and Services Tax payable or refundable.

To instil an attitude of professionalism and an awareness of ethical standards with regard to:

presentation of written work;

dealings with University staff;

interaction with peers; and

interaction with members of the professions.

To enhance verbal communication skills by encouraging students to participate in discussion and debate of legal issues.

To inspire students to approach the study of law with a keen and inquiring mind, and to maintain life-long interest in legal issues.

learning_outcomes.description: Prove a knowledge of the regulatory framework of the taxation system in Australia.

Demonstrate an understanding of the income tax equation and its elements and be able to apply those elements to the case of a taxpayer. Demonstrate an understanding of the operation of Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax.

Interpret provisions of the relevant Income Tax Assessment Act and other relevant legislation and apply them by preparing a concise written argument sufficient to form the basis of sound advice to a taxpayer and/or by calculating the taxation and financial implications of a particular transaction.
Critically evaluate case law as well as Rulings & Determinations issued by the Commissioner of Taxation.
Solve legal problems/issues relating to assessable income, deductions, capital gains tax, tax payable and tax offsets, Fringe Benefits Tax and the Goods and Services Tax.
Demonstrate the ability to research broad issues in the area of income tax law.
Discuss legal issues raised relating to taxation law in a confident and informed manner.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Work and Participation
assessment_details.task_description:
Number of tutorials during the semester

There are nine (9) tutorials.

Tutorial 1 and Tutorial 2: These tutorials are not part of the summative assessment in the course. Therefore, students do not have to submit answers to Tutorial 1 and Tutorial 2. But, there are tutorials classes for Tutorial 1 and Tutorial 2 during which we will discuss the answers to these tutorial tasks as part of Self Directed Learning. The tutorial classes for Tutorial 1 and Tutorial 2 will help you to better understand the course material and to prepare for the Mid Semester Exam.

Tutorial 3 to Tutorial 9: These seven tutorials form part of summative assessment in the course.

Tutorial 3 to Tutorial 9

The maximum marks that you can earn for tutorials during the semester is ten (10).

Tutorial 3 to Tutorial 9 are assessment pieces. Therefore, there are seven (7) tutorials that you can earn marks for.

The number of marks available for each individual tutorial (Tutorial 3 to Tutorial 9) is two-and-a-halve (2.5) marks.

Therefore, if a student meets all of the assessment criteria for four (4) of these seven tutorials, they will have obtained the maximum marks for tutorials.

Tutorial classes (on-line delivery only)

One tutorial class recording will be released to the course site on Learn.UQ for each tutorial. Students must watch and listen to the tutorial class recordings to compare their answers to the model answers. Therefore, each tutorial class recording is the method used to make the model answers available to students.

One additional recording will be released to the course site on Learn.UQ for each tutorial, that shares reflections on students' approaches to tutorial answers. Students must watch and listen to these recordings, as these discuss the most common errors in student answers.

Assessment criteria for Tutorial 3 to Tutorial 9

Each tutorial will comprise of more than one tutorial question or tasks. To meet the assessment criteria, students must prepare, complete, and type up answers to all the tutorial questions or tasks . In addition, the assessment criteria set out in each Study Guide issued for each topic covered in each tutorial must be met. Therefore, there are no part marks awarded for Tutorial 3 to Tutorial 9 .

The typed up answers to tutorials must be uploaded to the course site on Learn.UQ using the relevant TurnitIn links.

There is no specific font type, font size or spacing requirements that must be used to prepare typed up tutorial answers. But answers should be easy to read.

Students are to retain a copy of their tutorial answers.

Submission of tutorial answers for Tutorial 3 to Tutorial 9: Upload one file, being the typed up answers to all of the questions or tasks in a tutorial, saved as either a PDF document or as a Word document, in each separate folder created for each separate tutorial on the course site on Learn.UQ, using the relevant TurnitIn Link in the relevant teaching week in the Learning Resources folder. The UQ policy for penalties for late submission applies to the late submission of tutorial answers.

assessment_details.title: Mid Semester Take-home
assessment_details.task_description:
The Mid Semester Take-home will be released to the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ at 2 pm on Monday 20 April 2020 (Week 7). Students must upload their typed-up answers to the Mid Semester Take-home before 2 pm on Wednesday 22 April 2020 , using the Mid Semester Take-home upload link on the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ .

The Mid Semester Take-home takes the form of a number of questions that students have to answer. These questions are similar to the tutorial questions, in-class questions and case studies for the topics covered in this Take-home, but integrates all of the relevant topics, as compared to dealing with each topic separately.

It should take no longer than 2 1/2 hours (150 minutes) to prepare the answers to the Mid Semester Take-home.

Students must prepare their own answers.

Only typed-up answers uploaded to the course site will be accepted.

Students must prepare their answers independently from other students and without the assistance or input of other students and of other individuals.

The UQ academic integrity and plagiarism policies and procedures set out in section 6 of the course ECP applies to the Mid Semester Take-home.

Students must prepare for the Mid Semester Take-home by studying the relevant work covered in the Mid Semester Take-home as set out in each relevant topic Study Guide. It is essential to prepare for the Mid Semester Take-home in this way to ensure that you will be able to finish the Mid Semester Take-home in the approximately 150 required minutes to do so.

This is the work covered in the Mid Semester Take-home:

Introductory Study Guide and O-week folder on the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ.

Topics: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

The following folders on the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ: O-week; Week 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

Tutorials: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

As part of the answers uploaded to the course site, students are required to make the pledge as set out in the Mid Semester Take-home.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

Submission:

The Mid Semester Take-home must be submitted electronically on the course site in Learn.UQ. All answers must be typed up. One file in Word that contains the typed answers must be uploaded using the Mid Semester Take-home upload link.

assessment_details.title: Final Take-home
assessment_details.task_description:
The Final Take-home will be released to the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ at 2 pm on Monday 1 June 2020 (Week 13). Students must upload their typed-up answers to the Final Take-home before 2 pm on Thursday 4 June 2020 , using the Final Take-home upload link on the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ.

The Final Take-home takes the form of a number of questions that students have to answer. These questions are similar to the tutorial questions, in-class questions and case studies for the topics covered in this Take-home, but integrates all of the relevant topics, as compared to dealing with each topic separately. The Final Take-home will include questions that integrate aspects of Income Tax, Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax in single questions.

It should take no longer than 4 hours (240 minutes) to prepare the answers to the Final Take-home.

Students must prepare their own answers.

Only typed-up answers uploaded to the course site will be accepted.

Students must prepare their answers independently from other students and without the assistance or input of other students and of other individuals.

The UQ academic integrity and plagiarism policies and procedures set out in section 6 of the course ECP applies to the Final Take-home. Students will be required to make a pledge regarding the integrity of their Final Take-home.

Students must prepare for the Final Take-home during Week 1 to Week 12 of the semester by studying the relevant work covered in the Final Take-home as set out in each relevant topic Study Guide. Students who fail to prepare for the Final Take-home in this way will not be able to finish the Final Take-home in the approximately 240 required minutes to do so.

This is the work covered in the Final Take-home:

Introductory Study Guide and O-week folder on the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ.

Topics: 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10.

The following folders on the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ: Week 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12.

Tutorials: 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9.

learning_resources.required_resources:
Principles of Taxation Law (2020 edition). Thomson Reuters. Kerrie Sadiq et al. ISBN 9780455243115

As income tax law changes continuously, it is important that you use the 2020 edition of this textbook. The 2020 edition will be different to the 2019 edition.

You are not required to purchase any relevant legislation, or the 'Tax Kits'. You only have to purchase this one single textbook which is required as learning resource.

LAWS3101 Course site (BlackBoard).

In conjunction with Principles of Taxation Law 2020 , the LAWS3101 Course site on Learn.UQ (BlackBoard) contains the prescribed compulsory learning resources for this course.

Students must check the course site at least twice weekly.

Pay careful attention to the prescribed compulsory learning resources that you must study for each topic. These will be included in the Study Guides that will be released for each topic.

The Learning Resources folder contains the lecture recordings and course material, on a week-by-week basis, per topic.

Some topics are presented over more than one week, but one set of study guides, case studies and lectures slides will be made available per topic. Refer to the Learning Activities programme in section 4.1 of the course ECP.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
The following acts will be referenced during the course:

Income Tax Assessment Act 1997

Income tax Assessment Act 1936

A New Tax System (GST) Act 1999

Fringe Benefits Tax Assessment Act 1986.

These are available online:

Australian Legal Information Institute, www.austlii.edu.au

ATO, www.ato.gov.au

The relevant parts of the aforementioned legislation will be included in the study guides for the relevant topics, to supplement other learning resources.

Students may find resources such as the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) website, incorporating the ATO Legal Database helpful.

There is a useful list of internet resources in Principles of Taxation Law at [2.480].

A reference material folder is available for each topic on the course site on Learn.UQ. Each folder contains additional readings, but which is not examinable, incorporating relevant ATO Rulings and other documents relevant to each topic.

Australian Taxation Law Cases (2020). Thomson Reuters. Krever.

Students who wish to engage further with the cases referenced in Principles of Taxation Law will find this textbook useful, but this is not a required or prescribed textbook, since the case summaries in Principle of Taxation Law are sufficient.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS3101 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition) .

For a short summary of the complete Australian Guide to Legal Citation , see the UQ Library's Referencing Guide to the AGLC .

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Learn. UQ (Blackboard) Website

Course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the AskUs Library Help – askus@library.uq.edu.au .

The University of Queensland Library offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
The LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ contains all the necessary resources and information not included in the prescribed textbook.

Case studies for each Topic are available.

As there are no written exams scheduled for LAWS3101, no exam practice papers will be released.

The tutorials, in-class questions, study guides, textbook and case studies prepare students for the Mid Semester Take-home and Final Take-home.

learning_activities.topic: Mon Week 1 : Topic 1: The Taxation System in Australia. This topic introduces students to Income Tax, Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax. The topic then focuses on the Income Tax equation and a range of income-based taxes that are payable. The topic concludes with basic principles around tax avoidance and how the tax system in Australia is administered. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL 2020 (Chapter 1, 3, 15, 23 & 24); Course site (Week 1 - Topic 1)

Mon - Fri Tutorial 1 - Not an assessment piece (Self Directed Learning) : Tutorial 1 will be released in Week 1. Students are encouraged to prepare answers to Tutorial 1 so that they can maximise their learning experience in Week 2 when the answers to Tutorial 1 are discussed during tutorial classes. There is no on-line submission for Tutorial 1.

Mon Week 2 : Topic 2: Residence and source. This topic examines the key concepts of residence and source and how these impact income tax payable. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL 2020 (Chapter 4); Course site (Week 2 - Topic 2)
Mon - Thu Tutorial 1 : Material from Topic 1 Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6

Mon - Fri Tutorial 2 - Not an assessment piece (Self Directed Learning) : Tutorial 2 will be released in Week 2. Students are encouraged to prepare answers to Tutorial 2 so that they can maximise their learning experience in Week 3 when the answers to Tutorial 2 are discussed during tutorial classes. There is no on-line submission for Tutorial 2.

Mon Week 3 : Topic 3: Income. This topic examines the concept of income from employment, property and carrying on a business, and the tax accounting rules relevant to income. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL 2020 (Chapter 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 16, 21 & 23); Course site (Week 3 - Topic 3)
Mon - Thu Tutorial 2 : Material from Topic 2 Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7
Fri - Fri 14:00 Tutorial 3 - Online submission (Tutorial Series) : Typed answers to Tutorial 3 must be uploaded to the course site before Friday 2 pm using the Link in Week 3.
Mon - Fri UQ Pause Week (Study Module) : During this week, you should study Topic 1, Topic 2 and Topic 3 Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL 2020; Course site

Mon - Fri Week 4 : Topic 4: General and specific deductions. This topic examines a wide range of deductions, denied deductions, as well as tax accounting rules relevant to deductions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL 2020; Course site (Week 4 - Topic 4)
Wed - Wed 14:00 Tutorial 3 : Tutorial answer recording released at 2 pm on 25/03/20. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

Fri - Fri 14:00 Tutorial 4 - Online submission (Tutorial Series) : Typed answers to Tutorial 4 must be uploaded to the course site before Friday 2 pm using the TUT 4 upload link. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

Mon - Fri Week 5 : Topic 5: Capital allowances. This topic examines a wide range of capital allowances. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL 2020; Course site (Week 5 - Topic 5)
Wed - Wed 14:00 Tutorial 4 : Tutorial answer recording released at 2 pm on 1/04/2020. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

Fri - Fri 14:00 Tutorial 5 - Online submission (Tutorial Series) : Typed answers to Tutorial 5 must be uploaded to the course site before Friday 2 pm using the TUT 5 upload link. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

Mon - Thu Week 6 : Topic 6: Trading stock and a brief introduction to Topic 7: Capital Gains Tax. We examine trading stock and start to examine CGT. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL 2020; Course site (Week 6 and 8)

Mon - Fri MID SEMESTER BREAK : NO LECTURES. NO TUTORIALS. But assessment due - Tutorial 6

Tue - Tue 14:00 Tutorial 6 - Online submission (Tutorial Series) : Typed answers to Tutorial 6 must be uploaded to the course site before Tuesday 14/04/2020 2 pm using the TUT 6 upload link. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7
Mon 14:00 - Wed 14:00 Week 7: No lecture during this week : During Week 7, you have to submit your answers to the Mid Semester Take-home. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

Wed - Wed 14:00 Mid Semester Take-home (Mid Semester Take-home) : The Mid Semester Take-home has a weighting of 35 in your final mark for LAWS3101 (35%). Wed - Wed 14:00 Tutorial 9 : Tutorial answer recording released at 2 pm on 27/05/2020. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Tutorial - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Tutorial (AIT) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. All students are strongly encouraged to complete the module. Some course coordinators may require you to complete the module as part of your assessment.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the School’s policy ( https://law.uq.edu.au/files/7645/TL14-Feedback-on-final-examinations.pdf ).

Deferred Examinations ( mid-semester and end of semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with Section 6 of PPL 3.10.11 Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester examinations. Deferred end of semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to my.UQ for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Other Course Guidelines

The University of Queensland policies and procedures regarding plagiarism apply to submitted tutorial answers, and to the Mid Semester Take-home and Final Take-home.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
The overall aim of this introductory course in income tax law is to provide students with comprehensive knowledge of the basic principles of:

Income Tax, including an analysis of the concepts of income, assessable income, allowable deductions, capital gains, and the calculation of income tax and other levies which are based on taxable income

Goods and Services Tax

Fringe Benefits Tax in Australia

The interaction between these three taxes.

This course takes a practical approach to the relevant legislation, and requires of students to solve legal problems.

It is not the objective of this course to study all of the specific and technical provisions in the legislation which govern matters which affects the actual preparation of income tax returns and business activity statements.

Janine McMaster-Kirkwood Prize in Income Tax Law : The student with the best performance in their first attempt at LAWS3101 will be recommended for this prize. One prize will be awarded. This prize is not shared between students. The course coordinator makes the relevant recommendation in consultation with the Deputy Dean Academic at the Law School.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Some changes to the course material have been implemented to take into account prior year feedback. In particular:

Tax accounting, previously dealt with in Topic 2, has been incorporated into Topic 3 and Topic 4.

Topic 4 that previously covered a large range of deductions has now been split into Topic 4: General and specific deductions and Topic 5: Capital allowances.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
The function of Lectures

Lectures are a forum where, within an analytical framework offered by the lecturer, students and the lecturer can:

Explore concepts of law relevant to the topic

Explore how the particular concepts of law work, and relate to other concepts

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual, and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration.

The function of Tutorial classes

Tutorials offer students a forum within which to explore in more detail and in greater depth the issues introduced in lectures. Tutorials also offer students an opportunity to develop their skills in legal reasoning and argument.

Tutorials provide an inquiry rich forum and feedback opportunity. From Week 4, Tutorial feedback recordings will be released to the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ.

Mid Semester Take-home

In order to prepare for the Mid Semester Take-home, you should study the work covered in the Mid Semester Take-home as set out in each relevant topic Study Guide.

The Mid Semester Take-home should not take you more than 2 1/2 hours to complete if you have studied the work covered in the Mid Semester Take-home in advance.

Final Take-home

In order to prepare for the Final Take-home, you should study the work covered in the Final Take-home as set out in each relevant topic Study Guide.

The Final Take-home should not take you more than 4 hours to complete if you have studied the work covered in the Final Take-home in advance.

course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

85% +
late_submission:
Where a non-examination item of assessment (eg essays, assignments etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day that the assessment is overdue. The date and time of submission will be deemed to be that noted on the electronic submission.

Example: Where a piece of assessment is due at 2pm on Monday, assessment submitted after 2pm Monday but before 2pm on Tuesday will receive a 10% penalty.

Assessment submitted more than 5 days after the due date and time will receive zero marks.

Example: Where a piece of assessment is due at 2pm on the 10th, any work submitted after 2pm on the 15th will receive zero marks.

If the 5th day following the due date is a Saturday or a Sunday, then the date and time of submission will be the time the assessment was electronically submitted.

Example: A piece of assessment is due at 2pm on Monday the 9th. In the normal course of events, to receive any marks, a student must submit their assessment electronically by 2pm on Saturday the 14th (the 5th day following the due date) at the latest.

No extension granted may exceed 14 days.

For full details, students should refer to the Guidelines for Applying for an Extension . Except in exceptional circumstances, extensions for assessment will only be considered in respect of a certified medical condition . Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment unless the exceptional circumstance or medical condition is such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have applied prior to the due date.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.

Other Supplementary Assessment Information

Finalisation of grades for supplementary assessment

Where the supplementary is a written exam - unless the course profile states otherwise, the final grade awarded will be based on the results of the supplementary examination only, and a passing grade for the course will be awarded only if the student receives a passing grade or mark on the supplementary examination/s.

Where the supplementary assessment is not a written exam, the course profile will specify how the post-supplementary grade will be calculated.
